## Learning from Endometrial Cancer Research {#slide-1}

February 22, 2023

## Slide 2

![](ppt/media/image1.png "Picture 5")

## Slide 3

![](ppt/media/image2.png "Picture 4")

https://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-images/3ce36e4344afc09823195b45d8ca765191f2e0d0.png

## Slide 4

![](ppt/media/image3.png "Picture 3")

![Water with solid fill](ppt/media/image4.png "Graphic 5")

![Upward trend outline](ppt/media/image6.png "Graphic 7")

21%   BMI 22-27.2

43%   BMI27.5-29.5

273% BMI\>30

## Slide 5

![](ppt/media/image8.png "Picture 3")

Relative risk: 3.3

## Slide 6

![](ppt/media/image8.png "Picture 3")

![](ppt/media/image8.png "Picture 6")

Time

Odds ratio: 3.3

## Slide 7

![Diagram

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 3")

Cancer patients

Estrogen

Endometrial cancer

Community

For any estrogen use, OR=8

Mack et al (NEJM 1976) 

n=63

n=252

Estrogens and Endometrial Cancer in a Retirement Community

## Slide 8

![Diagram

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 3")

Endometrial Cancer patients

Estrogen

Endometrial cancer

Cancer patients

Other gynecologic cancer

OR = 11.98

Alternative analytic methods for case-control studies of estrogens and endometrial cancer

Horwitz et al (NEJM 1978) 

n=119

n=119

gynecologic cancer

n=35

n=84

n=115

n=4

29%

3%

## Slide 9

![Diagram

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 3")

Endometrial Cancer patients

Estrogen

Endometrial cancer

D&C patients

OR = 2.3

95%CI 1.26-4.25

P =.005

Alternative analytic methods for case-control studies of estrogens and endometrial cancer

Horwitz et al (NEJM 1978) 

D &C

Hysterectomy

n=149

n=149

n=44

n=105

n=126

n=23

30%

15%

## Slide 10

![Diagram

Description automatically generated](ppt/media/image9.png "Content Placeholder 3")

Endometrial Cancer patients

Estrogen

Endometrial cancer

D&C patients

OR = 1.7

95%CI 0.88-3.42

P =.123

Alternative analytic methods for case-control studies of estrogens and endometrial cancer

Horwitz et al (NEJM 1978) 

D &C

Hysterectomy

n=142

n=89

n=43

n=99

n=71

n=18

30%

20%

Stratified for bleeding

Uterine bleeding

Uterine bleeding

## Slide 11

![](ppt/media/image10.png "Picture 4")

Hulka  et al (NEJM 1980) 

RR = 3.9

RR = 3.7 

## Lessons learnt {#slide-12}

Selection bias

Especially when the selection condition is associated with exposure and outcome.

Detection bias

Multiple control groups

## Treatment {#slide-13}

Type I endometroid; type II serous

Stage I, II, III, IV

Histology grade 1-4

Low risk, intermediate risk, high risk

Survival, recurrence, quality of life

## Q1: surgery + radiation vs surgery alone {#slide-14}

![](ppt/media/image11.png "Picture 4")

## Timeline   treatment {#slide-15}

Portec-1 RCT Europe published in 2000

GOG99 RCT us published in 2004

Retrospective cohort study

Adopt evidence into clinical practice   published in 2013

  https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23376807/

Portec-2 RCT

Portec-3 RCT

## Slide 16

![](ppt/media/image12.png "Picture 3")

## Slide 17

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

No further treatment

Radiotherapy

5-year recurrence

Overall survival

Stage IB IC Endometrial cancer

TAH-BSO

1990

1997

## Slide 18

                         5-year Recurrence rate   5-year Survival rate   Endometrial cancer related Death rate   Complication rate
  ---------------------- ------------------------ ---------------------- --------------------------------------- -------------------
  Surgery alone          14%                      85%                    6%                                      6%
  Surgery+Radiotherapy   4%                       81%                    9%                                      25%

- Postoperative radiotherapy in stage-1 endometrial carcinoma reduces locoregional recurrence but has no impact on overall survival. 

n=360

n=354

## Slide 19

                         5-year Recurrence rate   Distant Metastases rate   Endometrial cancer related Death rate
  ---------------------- ------------------------ ------------------------- ---------------------------------------
  Surgery alone          18%                      5%                        8%
  Surgery+Radiotherapy   5%                       6%                        11%

- Postoperative radiotherapy reduces recurrence rate in high-risk patients
- Postoperative radiotherapy is not indicated in low-risk patients
- 

"high risk" category of patients:  60 and over with deeply invasive grade 1--2  tumours  or superficially invasive grade-3  tumours , 

## Q1: surgery + radiation vs surgery alone  {#slide-20}

![](ppt/media/image14.png "Picture 6")

## Slide 21

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

No further treatment

Radiotherapy

2-year recurrence

4-year survival

Stage IB IC stage II Endometrial cancer

TAH-BSO

1987

1995

## Slide 22

                         2-year Recurrence rate   4-year Survival rate
  ---------------------- ------------------------ ----------------------
  Surgery alone          12%                      86%
  Surgery+Radiotherapy   3%                       92%

- Adjunctive  radiotherapy  in early stage intermediate risk endometrial carcinoma decreases the risk of recurrence, but should be limited to patients whose risk factors fit a high intermediate risk definition . 

n=220

n=190

                         2-year Recurrence rate
  ---------------------- ------------------------
  Surgery alone          26%
  Surgery+Radiotherapy   6%

High-intermediate risk

## Slide 23

![](ppt/media/image15.png "Picture 4")

- For high-risk disease, pelvic RT continues to be the most effective adjuvant treatment.
- For low-risk disease, no adjuvant RT is indicated.
- Ultimate goal:  maximizing both event-free and complication-free survival.    
- Omitting RT increases the risk of recurrence. 
- pelvic RT vs vaginal brachytherapy?

## Q2:  P elvic RT vs vaginal brachytherapy   {#slide-24}

![](ppt/media/image16.png "Picture 4")

- Same efficacy in prevention 
- Fewer adverse effects
- Improved QOL

## Slide 25

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

External beam radiotherapy

VBRT

vaginal brachytherapy

VBT

Vaginal recurrence

Stage IB IC Stage II Endometrial cancer of high-intermediate risk

TAH-BSO

2002

2008

n=214

n=213

Median follow-up: 45 months

## Slide 26

         5-year Recurrence rate   5-year Survival rate   GI toxicity rate
  ------ ------------------------ ---------------------- ------------------
  EBRT   1.6%(0.5-4.9)            79.6%(71.2-88.0)       53.8%
  VBT    1.8%(0.6-5.9)            84.8%(79.3-90.3)       12.6%

- VBT is effective in ensuring vaginal control, with fewer gastrointestinal toxic effects than with EBRT. 
- 
- VBT should be the adjuvant treatment of choice for patients with endometrial carcinoma of high-intermediate risk.

## Q2:  P elvic RT vs vaginal brachytherapy   {#slide-27}

![](ppt/media/image17.png "Picture 4")

2009

## Slide 28

![](ppt/media/image13.png "Picture 3")

External beam radiotherapy

vaginal brachytherapy

2-year QOL

Stage IB IC Stage II Endometrial cancer of high-intermediate risk

TAH-BSO

2002

2008

n=214

n=213

Baseline QOL

## Slide 29

Patients in the VBT group reported better social functioning and lower symptom scores . 

2012: published 5-year QOL results

VBT provides better  long-term health related quality of life

2015: published 7+ year QOL results

VBT group reported less bowl symptoms and less urinary symptoms.

No difference in overall QOL and cancer survivorship.

## Slide 30

![](ppt/media/image15.png "Picture 4")

- For high-risk disease, pelvic RT continues to be the most effective adjuvant treatment.
- For low-risk disease, no adjuvant RT is indicated.
- Ultimate goal:  maximizing both event-free and complication-free survival.    
- Omitting RT increases the risk of recurrence. pelvic RT vs vaginal brachytherapy?

## Slide 31

![](ppt/media/image18.png "Picture 4")

"within the HIR population, there may be stronger evidence of a decreased recurrence rate with the use of adjuvant radiation, leading us to expect an uptake of radiation use in this population."

2013

## Slide 32

![](ppt/media/image19.png "Picture 1")

https://sphweb.bumc.bu.edu/otlt/MPH-Modules

## Slide 33

![](ppt/media/image20.png "Picture 3")

https://sites.pitt.edu/\~super1/lecture/lec19101/img038.JPG

2004-2007

Radiation rate

50.9%

48.2%

n=2764

n=1960

Stage I

Stage II

High-Intermediate Risk

2000-2007

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23376807/

2000-2003

Radiation rate

The rate of adjuvant radiation in the US did not increase after publication of PORTEC1 and GOG99

## Slide 34

![](ppt/media/image21.png "Picture 3")

2015

Low risk: surveillance 

High-intermediate risk: VBT

? Chemoradiation vs chemo 

GOG258 

PORTEC 3

## 2013-2022 {#slide-35}

Histopathological characteristics

Molecular phenotypes

![](ppt/media/image22.png "Picture 6")

Endometroid -- type I

Other -- type II

## Slide 36

![](ppt/media/image23.png "Picture 4")

2013

Editorial Summary

This might have clinical relevance for post-surgical adjuvant treatment of women with aggressive  tumours .

![](ppt/media/image24.png "Picture 8")

FDA   approved the programmed death-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab for any patient with mismatch repair deficiency and high microsatellite instability. 

## Slide 37

![](ppt/media/image25.png "Picture 4")

2020

- Performance of next-generation sequencing in biopsy specimens from patients with relapsed-refractory advanced permits triaging nearly one-fifth of patients to evidence-based investigational therapy.

MATCH   is a  precision medicine  cancer treatment  clinical trial (2015- ) . 

In this trial, people with cancer are assigned to receive treatment based on the  genetic  changes found in their tumors rather than the type of cancer.

## Slide 38

![](ppt/media/image26.png "Picture 5")

2022

## Slide 39

![](ppt/media/image27.png "Picture 4")

## Slide 40

![](ppt/media/image28.png "Picture 4")

## Q3:  chemoRT  vs RT {#slide-41}

![](ppt/media/image29.png "Picture 4")

## Slide 42

![](ppt/media/image30.png "Picture 4")

## Slide 43

![](ppt/media/image31.png "Content Placeholder 3")

## Slide 44

https://www.cancer.gov/about-nci/organization/ccg/research/structural-genomics/tcga/studied-cancers/uterine

https://www.annalsofoncology.org/article/S0923-7534(22)01207-8/pdf

https://www.cancer.gov/types/uterine/research
